content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 6, 2006

Michael Reiter
Assistant City Attorney
Office of the City Attorney
City of Redlands
P. O. Box 3005
Redlands, CA 92373-1505

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-191

Dear Mr. Reiter:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Mayor Pro Tem Patricia Gilbreath for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your question is general in nature and does not refer to a specific factual situation, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place.

QUESTION

You ask whether retirement gifts from Mayor Pro Tem Gilbreath's employer and presents from clients may be reported as income.

CONCLUSION

Retirement gifts from Mayor Pro Tem Gilbreath's employer and clients must be reported as gifts and not as income. See discussion below.

FACTS

Mayor Pro Tem Patricia Gilbreath will retire at the end of this calendar year from Eadie & Payne, LLP, an accounting firm that does business in Redlands. Ms. Gilbreath

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).

File No. I-06-191
Page No. 2

expects that she will receive "retirement gifts" from coworkers, clients, and from the
firm. In the past, "retirement gifts" from Eadie & Payne, LLP, have included large
screen televisions, golf clubs, and cruise line gift certificates, but not cash.

You wish to know whether the gift limitations in Government Code section 89503
applies to retirement gifts given to Ms. Gilbreath by her employer, Eadie & Payne, LLP,
and whether such gifts may be reported as income. You also wish to know whether
retirement gifts from Ms. Gilbreath's clients may be reported as income, instead of gifts.

ANALYSIS

The Act imposes certain restrictions and obligations on public officials regarding
the receipt of gifts. As Mayor Pro Tem for the City of Redlands, Patricia Gilbreath is an
elected officer as that term is defined under section 82020 of the Act.

Section 89503 provides that no elected state officer, elected officer of a local
government agency, or other individual specified in section 87200 may accept gifts from
any single source in any calendar year worth in excess of the gift limit if the official
would be required to report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her
statement of economic interests. The current gift limit is $360. (Regulation 18940.2.)

In addition, an elected state officer is required to disclose, on an annual statement
of economic interests, the name and address of each source of gifts aggregating $50 or
more received in a calendar year. (Sections 87200, 87202-87203 and 87207.) As an
elected officer, Mayor Pro Tem Gilbreath is subject to these provisions.

Section 82028 (a) defines "gift" as "any payment that confers a personal benefit
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received
and includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public without
regard to official status."

There are certain exceptions to the definition of "gift" under the Act, including,
among others: informational material, gifts that are not used and returned within 30 days
of receipt, and gifts from certain enumerated family members. (Section 82028(b).)

You state in your letter that, "In the past, 'retirement gifts' from Eadie & Payne,
LLP have included large screen televisions, golf clubs and cruise line gift certificates, but
no cash." You also state that Ms. Gilbreath expects that she will receive retirement gifts
from co-workers and clients of the firm.

You ask whether such gifts may be viewed as consideration for Ms. Gilbreath's
years of service on behalf of her employer, and whether gifts from clients may be viewed
as income.

File No. I-06-191
Page No. 3


Your facts do not indicate that there was any formal company policy regarding
such gifts as consideration for employment.³ You also do not provide any other details
with regard to potential gifts from Ms. Gilbreath's clients. As a result, there is no basis
for a conclusion that such gifts can be considered part of an employee's compensation
package.

Payments, in the form of retirement presents—either from a corporation, such as
Ms. Gilbreath's employer, or her clients—absent an exception,⁴ are considered gifts
under the Act. Therefore, based on the facts you have provided, such presents do not
appear to fall under any exemption under the Act, and thus would be subject to gift
reporting rules and gift limits.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

ER:meh
I:\AdviceLtrs\06-191

³ In the past, the Commission has advised that certain fringe benefits are part of an employee's
salary in that an employee accrues to receive a smaller payment in exchange for receiving the remaining
portion of his other compensation by way of these fringe benefits. These have included fringe benefits
such as pensions, health benefits, deferred compensation, tax sheltered annuities, life insurance, leave,
etc. (In re Moore (1976) 3 FPPC Ops. 33, 36.) You have not provided any information that would indicate
such retirement gifts would be considered "fringe benefits" of Ms. Gilbreath's employment. Therefore,
such payments would be considered gifts, and not income.

⁴ Section 89503(e) provides that gifts exchanged between individuals (other than lobbyists) on
holidays, birthdays, or other similar occasions are not prohibited or limited as long as the gifts exchanged
are not substantially disproportionate in value. Regulation 18942(a)(8) provides that gifts exchanged
between an official and any other individual (except a lobbyist) on holidays, birthdays, or similar
occasions, which are not substantially disproportionate in value, are not subject to limits and need not be
reported as gifts on the official's statement of economic interests. Recent amendments to this regulation
were intended to be broad enough to cover a range of occasions such as weddings, retirement parties, the
birth, adoption, or baptism of a child, or even memorial services, where gifts are customarily exchanged.
Thus, if Ms. Gilbreath "exchanges" gifts (as provided under regulation 18942(a)(8)) with individuals who
provide her with retirement presents, such gifts would not be subject to reporting rules or the annual gift
limit of $360 per source. Please note, however, that this exception does not apply to gifts provided by
lobbyists or non-individuals, such as corporations and organizations.

reported as gifts and not as income. see discussion below.

reported as gifts and not as income. see discussion below.
